label: Yirmeyahu (Jeremiah) — Part 6 — Chapters 40–48
summary_plain: After the fall of Yerushalayim, Nebukhadnetzar leaves Gedalyahu son of Achikam as governor over the remnant in the land. Yirmeyahu is released from chains and stays with Gedalyahu at Mitzpah. Yishma'EL son of Netanyahu assassinates Gedalyahu and kills the garrison. Yochanan son of Qareach pursues Yishma'EL and rescues the captives. The remnant asks Yirmeyahu to inquire of YHWH — he says: Stay in this land and do not go down to Mitsrayim. But they refuse to obey YHWH's voice and flee to Mitsrayim, taking Yirmeyahu with them. In Mitsrayim, the women burn incense to the Queen of Heaven and say: When we burned incense to her, we had plenty of food and were well. Yirmeyahu delivers oracles against Mitsrayim, Moav, Ammon, Edom, Damascus, Qedar, Chatsor, and Elam.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from the DSS Jeremiah manuscripts (4QJer-a through 4QJer-e) and the Orit Ge'ez. Chapters 40-44 document the aftermath of the Babylonian destruction — the remnant community's decision to flee to Mitsrayim against YHWH's explicit command. The Queen of Heaven worship in chapter 44 records the women's defense of syncretic practice — they claim prosperity came through her veneration, not through YHWH's covenant. The LXX arrangement of Jeremiah differs significantly from the Masoretic in these chapters — the oracles against the nations (chapters 46-48 in the MT) appear in a different position in the LXX, and the DSS fragments support the shorter LXX text type. The flight to Mitsrayim inverts the exodus pattern: the covenant people voluntarily return to the house of bondage.

key_terms.definition: Son of Achikam, appointed by Nebukhadnetzar as governor over the remnant left in the land after the fall of Yerushalayim. He was assassinated by Yishma'EL son of Netanyahu at Mitzpah. His murder ended the last vestige of Judean self-governance in the land.

A pagan deity worshipped by Judean women in Mitsrayim (44:17-19). The women claim prosperity came through burning incense and pouring libations to her. Yirmeyahu identifies this as the reason YHWH brought calamity. Often identified with Ishtar/Ashtoreth — the same fertility goddess tradition later absorbed into Marian devotion.
The administrative center where Gedalyahu governed the remnant after the fall of Yerushalayim. Site of the assassination and the gathering of the remnant who then fled to Mitsrayim.

fill_blank.prompt: If you will remain in this ______, then I will build you up and not pull you down — I will plant you and not pluck you up.
fill_blank.source_quote: If you will remain in this land, then I will build you up and not pull you down — I will plant you and not pluck you up, for I relent of the disaster that I did to you.

fill_blank.prompt: We will burn incense to the Queen of ______ and pour out drink offerings to her, as we did, we and our fathers.
fill_blank.source_quote: We will do everything that we have vowed — burn incense to the Queen of Heaven and pour out drink offerings to her, as we did, we and our fathers, our kings and our officials.

fill_blank.prompt: Behold, I release you today from the ______ on your hands.
fill_blank.source_quote: Behold, I release you today from the chains on your hands. If it seems good to you to come with me to Babylon, come, and I will look after you.

multiple_choice.question: Who assassinated Gedalyahu the governor?
multiple_choice.source_quote: Yishma'EL son of Netanyahu, son of Elishama, of the royal family, came with ten men to Gedalyahu at Mitzpah. And they ate bread together at Mitzpah. Then Yishma'EL son of Netanyahu rose up and struck down Gedalyahu.

multiple_choice.question: What did YHWH command the remnant through Yirmeyahu, and what did they do?
options: Stay in the land — and they obeyed
Go to Babylon — and they went
Stay in the land — but they fled to Mitsrayim
Go to Mitsrayim — and they went
multiple_choice.source_quote: YHWH has said to you, remnant of Yehudah: Do not go to Mitsrayim. But Yochanan and all the commanders took the entire remnant and went to the land of Mitsrayim.

multiple_choice.question: What did the women in Mitsrayim claim about the Queen of Heaven?
options: When they worshipped her they had plenty of food and were well
She commanded them to leave the land
She was the mother of the prophets
multiple_choice.source_quote: When we burned incense to the Queen of Heaven and poured out drink offerings to her, we had plenty of food and prospered and saw no disaster.

faq.question: What does the Queen of Heaven worship reveal about the Great Eraser?
faq.answer: In Yirmeyahu 44, the women defend their worship of the Queen of Heaven (Melekhet HaShamayim) — claiming that prosperity came through her veneration. Yirmeyahu condemns this as the reason YHWH brought calamity. The Queen of Heaven is identified across comparative religion scholarship with Ishtar/Ashtoreth — the Mesopotamian-Canaanite fertility goddess. The Roman Church later absorbed the same devotional structure — a heavenly queen receiving prayers, incense, and petitions — into the Marian cult. The Council of Ephesus (431 CE) declared Mary 'Theotokos' (Mother of God), and the veneration practices that followed — incense, images, prayers for intercession, the rosary — structurally reproduce the Queen of Heaven devotion that YHWH explicitly condemned through Yirmeyahu. The Great Eraser did not merely suppress the covenant people's identity — it absorbed pagan practices the prophets condemned and re-branded them as Christian doctrine.

faq.question: Why does the flight to Mitsrayim invert the exodus pattern?
faq.answer: YHWH brought the covenant people out of Mitsrayim with signs and wonders — the defining act of the covenant. In Yirmeyahu 42-43, the remnant voluntarily returns to Mitsrayim against YHWH's explicit command: 'Do not go to Mitsrayim.' They go back to the house of bondage by choice. The entire Exodus — the foundational event of covenant identity — is reversed. The remnant prefers the security of the oppressor's land to the risk of obeying YHWH in their own land. This pattern recurs: covenant people choosing institutional security within systems that dominate them rather than trusting YHWH's direct instruction. The Damascus Document at Qumran describes the Period of Wrath as a time when the people walked in the stubbornness of their hearts — the same language Yirmeyahu uses for the remnant who fled to Mitsrayim.

faq.question: What do the DSS fragments reveal about Yirmeyahu's text in these chapters?
faq.answer: The 4QJer fragments preserve a text approximately one-eighth shorter than the Masoretic version of Yirmeyahu. The LXX Jeremiah is similarly shorter and arranges the oracles against the nations differently — they appear after 25:13 in the LXX rather than in chapters 46-51 as in the MT. The DSS fragments support the LXX text type, demonstrating that the shorter version is not a Greek abbreviation but reflects an earlier Hebrew Vorlage. The Masoretic text represents an expanded edition. The ACR prioritizes the DSS and Orit Ge'ez readings over the Masoretic expansion — the older witnesses take precedence over the 10th-century standardization.
